coredata.dc:title: A nanosized photothermal responsive core-shell carbonized polymer dots based on poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) for light-triggered drug release
coredata.dc:creator: Forte, Giuseppe
Consiglio, Giuseppe
Satriano, Cristina
Maugeri, Ludovica
Petralia, Salvatore
coredata.dc:description: Core-shell nanocomposites are one of the most important achievements in the fast-growing field of nanotechnology. The combination of multi-responsive nano-shell with luminescent and photothermal core has led to promising applications in various fields such as optics, electronics and medicine. In this work, a nanosized core-shell system composed by carbonized dots core and poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) shell was developed and the photothermal triggered release of doxorubicin was demonstrated. The system was fully characterized by H1-NMR, DLS, Z-potential, AFM, optical absorption and fluorescence measurements. A photothermal conversion efficiency (η) value of about 67.9% and a doxorubicin photo-release rate value of about 1.0% min−1 were measured. Molecular dynamic (MD) simulations data were in agreement with experimental results, at 310 K the coil-to-globule transition and a consequent desorption of doxorubicin from the polymer were observed. Both the radius of gyration and the fluctuation of the distance doxorubicin-PNIPAM pointed that the temperature above the LCST and the acid pH facilitated the polymer transition. Moreover, MD simulations and experimental data suggested an influence on the lower critical solution temperature (LCST) exerted by the number of polymer chains anchored to the carbon core.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photothermal
Luminescent nanomaterials
Molecular dynamic
DLS, zeta potential
AFM

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dynamic light scattering and Z-potential characterization

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research activity has been developed under the INCREASE project, funded under Action Linea Piaceri- STARTING GRANT 2020 , University of Catania , Italy. Special thanks to Prof. M. Teresa Musumeci for the use of DLS, Z-potential and spectrofluorometer instruments and to provide the doxorubicin for the photothermal-triggered release experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CPDs-PNIPAM: A) A representative AFM topography image on a 1 µm scan size area (z scale = 30 nm); B) the corresponding height section profile; C) optical absorption spectrum in water and D) emission fluorescence spectra at various excitation wavelength (300, 350, 400 and 450 nm) in water.

A) Hydrodynamic size measurements by DLS for CPDs-PNIPAM (core-shell ratio 1:3 and 1:10) at various temperature (25 °C, 30 °C, 35 °C and 40 °C) and various pH conditions: core-shell ratio 1:3, pH 7.0 (red circle), core-shell ratio 1:3, pH 5.0 ( black square), core-shell ratio 1:10, pH 7.0 (blue triangles), core-shell ratio 1:10, pH 5.0 (blue triangles) (B) Photographs of CPDs-PNIPAM (ratio 1:3, pH 5.0) at 24 °C (t < LCST) and 40 °C (t > LCST).
Z-potential measurements for CPDs and CPD-PNIPAM at pH values 7.0) and 5.0, and at various CPDs-PNIPAM ratio 1:3 and 1:10.

Photothermal behavior: A) Representative thermos-photographs of CPDs-PNIPAM during the photothermal experiments, B) photothermal effect of CPDs-PNIPAM (2.5 mg mL−1) and C) time constant τs from the system.

CPDs-PNIPAM-doxo: A) optical absorption spectra for CPDs-PNIPAM/doxo in water at pH5.0 (line 1), fluorescence emission spectra for CPDs-PNIPAM/doxo at various wavelength excitation λ
exc = 360 nm (line 2) and λ
exc = 510 nm (line 3) and B) percentage of doxorubicin released from CPDs-PNIPAM/doxo pH 5.0 at various conditions: room temperature (24 °C from t = 0 min t = 25 min), 30 °C (below LCST value, from t = 25 to t = 40 min), upon light irradiation (λlaserc.405 nm) (above LCST value t = 40–55 min), 30 °C (t = 55–75 min) and upon light irradiation (λlaserc.405 nm) t = 75–95 min.

Rg trends for CPDs-PNIPAM-doxo at temperature a) below and b) above the LCST and pH values of 7.0 and 5.0; Root mean square deviation for CPDs-PNIPAM-doxo at temperature c) below and d) above LCST and at different pH; time fluctuation of the average distance between doxorubicin molecules and PNIPAM chain at different conditions at temperature e) below and f) above LCST and at different pH.

MD simulation: A) Snapshots of CPD6 under acid condition at 310 K taken at 0 ns, 50 ns and 150 ns; polymer chains are shown in different colors (stick representation), the central carbon dot is reported in white (stick representation) whereas doxorubicin molecules are shown in CPK representation. For clarity water molecules are omitted; B) c(t) autocorrelation function for hydrogen bond involving PNIPAM acceptor groups and H atoms of solvent molecules.

head.ce:title: A nanosized photothermal responsive core-shell carbonized polymer dots based on poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) for light-triggered drug release

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Drug Science and Health, University of Catania, Via S. Sofia, 64, 95125 Catania, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Drug Science and Health, University of Catania

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Drug Science and Health, University of Catania, Via S. Sofia, 64, 95125 Catania, Italy;

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Science, University of Catania, Via S. Sofia, 64, 95125 Catania, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Science, University of Catania

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Science, University of Catania, Via S. Sofia, 64, 95125 Catania, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: A.O.U Policlinico “G. Rodolico San Marco”, Via S. Sofia, 5125 Catania, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: A.O.U Policlinico “G. Rodolico San Marco” Via S. Sofia, 5125 Catania, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Core-shell nanocomposites are one of the most important achievements in the fast-growing field of nanotechnology. The combination of multi-responsive nano-shell with luminescent and photothermal core has led to promising applications in various fields such as optics, electronics and medicine. In this work, a nanosized core-shell system composed by carbonized dots core and poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) shell was developed and the photothermal triggered release of doxorubicin was demonstrated. The system was fully characterized by H1-NMR, DLS, Z-potential, AFM, optical absorption and fluorescence measurements. A photothermal conversion efficiency (η) value of about 67.9% and a doxorubicin photo-release rate value of about 1.0% min−1 were measured. Molecular dynamic (MD) simulations data were in agreement with experimental results, at 310 K the coil-to-globule transition and a consequent desorption of doxorubicin from the polymer were observed. Both the radius of gyration and the fluctuation of the distance doxorubicin-PNIPAM pointed that the temperature above the LCST and the acid pH facilitated the polymer transition. Moreover, MD simulations and experimental data suggested an influence on the lower critical solution temperature (LCST) exerted by the number of polymer chains anchored to the carbon core.

ce:section.ce:para: In these last years, continued efforts have been focused on the development of nanomaterials that hold promise in photothermal therapy (PTT) and, most recently, in chemo-photothermal combination therapy (chemo-PTT), which represents a new frontier for the cancer therapy application [1–3], in this scenario various nanoplatforms for chemo-PTT were developed and proposed. Recently, Wang et al. have reported a metal-organic framework based on Iron nanoparticles coloaded with two anticancer drugs, oxaliplatin and indocyanine, as chemo-PTT agent upon NIR-light exposition [4]. Moon and coworker proposed a photothermal responsive gold-based polydopamine nano-system combined with doxorubicin as chemo-PTT agents against primary and disseminated tumors [5]. Recently, an interesting review was published by Tekadea et al., they highlight the role of nanomaterials and nanoplatforms in chemo-PTT [6]. Metal and metal oxide nanomaterials are the most investigated photothermal agents in anti-cancer treatment, but their long-term toxicity, low stability and high cost drastically have limited their biomedical applications. [7–10]. To overcome these drawbacks light-responsive carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon dots (CDs), carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), carbon quantum dots and graphene oxide have aroused intensive interest in broad fields including bioimaging [11], sensing [12], theragnostic [13], drug delivery [14,15], and photodynamic therapy [16]. Recently, it has been demonstrated that the optical absorption as well the multicolor emission of CDs are related to the size effects [17]. Moreover, the surface state induced emission can be easily tuned by the presence of doping agents such as nitrogen, boron, and oxygen [18].

By today, few examples of carbon-based chemo-PTT agents are reported in literature [19], in this context, nanocomposites made of a carbonized polymer core and nanosized shell composed by materials such as polymers, biomolecules are very fascinating systems for a huge of technical applications [20,21]. These systems combine the optical and photothermal properties of the carbon core with the multi-functionality of the shell, resulting in multi-responsive core-shell nano-systems. The poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) due to its chemical and optical stability, together with the temperature and pH-responsive characteristics, is largely used for biomedical applications as drug delivery agents [22] and sensing materials [23]. It exhibits in aqueous solution a lower critical solution temperature (LCST) value in the range of 30–33 °C, becoming hydrophobic and less water-soluble at temperature above the LCST.

A plethora of MD simulations studies have been performed in order to clarify the coil-to-globule transition mechanism of PNIPAM. To this purpose the parameters influencing the LCST, various force fields and a wide range of chain lengths have been considered [24–27].

MD simulations performed by Kang et al. suggested that transition temperatures depend on the initial conformation [28]. Li and co-workers realized a functionalized graphene sheets by grafting PNIPAM demonstrating that PNIPAM- graphene sheets system is able to interacts and deliver aromatic and water-insoluble drug [29]. Recently our team has performed MD simulations study to evaluate the influence of silicon and carbon-based surfaces with different coverage density of hydroxyl groups on LCST value for an oligomer chain of PNIPAM. We demonstrated that a higher hydroxyl groups coverage increases the LCST value of about 4–5 °C [30]. Bejagam et al. have employed a new computational framework which integrates coarse-grained, molecular dynamics simulations and a data driven machine-learning method to study complex processes such as transitions in stimuli-sensitive polymers [31]. Computational studies performed on PNIPAM-graphene oxide surface have shown that thermal sensitivity induces an on-off surface state, giving rise to a stronger interaction between aptamer and protein at 310 k compared to 298 K [32].

Motivated by the above considerations, we have developed a water soluble, blue-luminescent and photothermal responsive nanosized core-shell which combines the optical and photothermal effects of the CPDs “core” with the pH- and thermo-responsive properties of PNIPAM “shell”. The CPDs core was prepared by a simple process recently developed in our laboratory [33]. Subsequently, uniform PNIPAM shell was created by EDC/NHS condensation between the amino termination of PNIPAM chain with the carboxylic groups of CPDs surface. The as prepared CPDs-PNIPAM core-shell was fully characterized and the photothermal release of doxorubicin was demonstrated. MD simulations performed for drug-release system at different pH conditions and temperature confirm that the coil-to-globule transition of the polymer is the key for the doxorubicin release mechanism release. All results show that above LCST the folding and the consequent drug desorption are facilitated by acid pH compared to a neutral pH.
Amino terminated PNIPAM (5.500 Da) and doxorubicin were purchased by Sigma-Aldrich. All reagents were purchased by Sigma-Aldrich and used as received.

Optical absorption UV-Vis spectra were acquired by spectrophotometer Perkin Elmer 365, the Photoluminescence (PL) spectra at different excitation wavelengths were measured by a spectrofluorometer equipment. A quartz cuvette with optical length of 10 mm was used.
The particle size and surface charge were examined in water by using a combined dynamic light scattering and Zeta-potential apparatus (DLS, Zetasizer Marvell).

H1-NMR experiments were performed on a Varian Unity S 500 (499.88 MHz for 1H) spectrometer and referenced to HDO signal. All NMR measurements were performed using a solution containing 50 % v/v of D2O in Milli-Q water. Water signal was partially suppressed using diffusion experiments. 1H-DOSY spectra were recorded at 27 and 40 °C. Dbppste pulse-sequence was selected for acquiring diffusion spectra. The Varian DOSY package was used to acquire and process all spectra (VnmrJ® version 4.2 revision C).

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) imaging was performed in AC mode in air on a commercial AFM instrument (Cypher, Oxford Instruments, Santa Barbara, CA) equipped with a scanner at an XY scan range of 30/40 µm (closed/open loop). Silicon Cantilevers (OMCL-AC240TS, ~ 70 kHz, 2 N/m by Olympus, Japan) were used to acquire scan images in random areas of the samples. To prepare the samples, an 10 μL volume of CPDs (10 mg mL−1) was dispensed on freshly cleaved muscovite mica (Ted Pella, Inc., Redding, CA, USA) and left to dry at room temperature in controlled laboratory environment. AFM images of height were analyzed using the free tool in the MFP-3DTM offline analysis software.

The CPDs were prepared from poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) using a method recently developed in our laboratories [33]. In details, an amount of 50 mg of PNIPAM was pyrolyzed for 4 h at 300 °C to obtain carbon-based materials. The dark-brown solid was washed with 1 mL of milliQ-water to eliminate the excess of reagent and by-products and finally dispersed in 1 mL of acetone, then was sonicated for 2 min. The dispersion was separated by centrifugation (8.000 rpm 15 min), the brown supernatant was dried and then treated with a sodium hydroxide solution. After neutralization the solution was filtered through 0.2 µm microfilter. The yellowish dispersion of CPDs was so obtained and used.

The CPDs-PNIPAM dispersion was prepared adding to an aliquot of CPDs dispersion (volume 15 mL, concentration 1.0 mg mL−1 and pH = 4) an amount of 17.1 mg of NHS and 25.7 mg of EDC, the mixture was stirred for 60 min. Then, amino terminated PNIPAM was added and the mixture was stirred at room temperature overnight. Two CPDs-PNIPAM weight-ratio (1:3 and 1:10) were prepared, using 27 and 90 mg of amino-terminated PNIPAM respectively. The resulting dispersion were dialyzed using MilliQ-water through a dialysis membrane (6–8 kDa cut off).

An aliquot of 2 mL of CPDs-PNIPAM dispersion was mixed with 200 μL of doxorubicin solution (1 mg mL−1) and stirred for 24 h at room temperature. In order to remove the excess of doxorubicin the solution was dialyzed (3.5 kDa cut off) for 24 h in water.

Photothermal properties of CPDs-PNIPAM were investigated by irradiating a glass tube (diameter 3 mm) containing various amount of the CPDs-PNIPAM dispersion (0.4–4.2 mg mL−1). A volume of 100 μL of CPDs-PNIPAM dispersion was irradiated with a CW laser 405 nm (power 158 mW) for 10 min. We used the Flirck infrared thermal imaging camera to measure the temperature of solution every 30 s, during the heating and cooling processes. Photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of CPDs-PNIPAM was calculated according to Eq. (1) introduced by Roper [34].
(1)
η =
hA
T
max
−
T
surr
−
Q
Dis
I ( 1 −
10
− A
)
Where Tmax (42.0 °C) and Tsurr (24.0 °C) represents the max photothermal temperature and the ambient temperature respectively. The absorbance (A) of CPDs-PNIPAM at 405 nm is 0.5. The incident 405 nm laser power 0.185 W cm−2.

Then, Eqs. (2) and (3) were introduced to obtain unknow hA.
(2)
ϴ =
T −
T
surr
T
max
−
T
surr
(3)
τ =
M
D
C
D
hA
where MD and CD are the mass of water (0.1 g) and heat capacity (4.2 J g−1) of water, respectively, and τs is the time constant.

The time constant τs was calculated by the Eq. (4).
(4)
t
=
−
τ ( ln ϴ )

To assess the photothermal triggered release of doxorubicin from CPDs-PNIPAM-doxo, aliquots of 600 μL at pH 5.0 was dispensed on micromembrane for dialysis (cut off 3.5–5 kDa) and dipped in 1.7 mL of water at pH 5.0 in a standard quartz spectroscopic cuvette. The solution was maintained under stirring during all the experiments. The temperature of CPDs-PNIPAM-doxo dispersion was measured by thermo-camera. The solution (1.7 mL of water at pH 5.0) was replaced after each laser exposition. Fluorescence measurements were performed after various laser exposition time (λexcitation = 450 nm and fluorescence range 460–750 nm).

For hemolysis assay experiments, human whole blood obtained from volunteer (5 mL) was collected and added to PBS solution (10 mL). The red blood cells (RBCs) were isolated from plasma by centrifugation at 500 rpm for 10 min and washed with PBS for five time. The RBCs suspension (0.5 mL) was added to CPDs-PNIPAM solution with concentration 0.1, 0.6 and 1 mg mL−1. The negative control was PBS while the positive control was deionized water. The percent hemolisys was calculated by absorbance value measured at 540 nm using the following equation:
Percent
hemolisys
%
=
Abs .
SAMPLE
− Abs .
Negative
CTRL
Abs .
Positive
CTRL − Abs .
Negative
CTRL
* 100

The adopted models were built by using the Biovia Discovery Studio, in particular a spherical core of carbon atoms [35] was covalently bonded respectively with 6 oligomer chains of 40-mer PNIPAM and two molecules of doxorubicin were placed on each chain so that a physisorption interaction occurs between polymer and molecules. 40-mer is considered as an optimal value for the study of the polymer phase transition. The obtained system, henceforth named CDP6, was placed in a box of 20 × 19×19 nm, solvated with water molecules and equilibrated with a procedure consisting of 30.000 steps minimization of steepest descent algorithm followed by 10 ns MD (SI). Finally, further 200 ns of MD simulation was performed, storing atomic coordinates every 2.0 ps for analysis.

Different conditions of both temperature and pH were considered during the simulation, in particular we simulate the systems below (298 K) and above (310 K) the polymer LCST at neutral and acid pH. In this case both the nitrogen atom of doxorubicin and the oxygen atoms of PNIPAM were protonated and Cl- counterions were added to maintain the electric neutrality.

All the simulation were performed under periodic boundary condition in NPT ensemble (P = 1 atm, and Berendsen barostat), employing the polymer consistent force field (PCFF) included in the Material Studio package. Furthermore, a PCFF water model, whose application has shown good agreement with experimental data, was used [36–39].
A time step of 1 fs was set to integrate the equation of motion and the Ewald summation method was used to calculate long-range electrostatic interactions.

The root means square deviations, RMSDs, of the generate trajectories, compared to the initial structure were computed as follow:
RMSD
t
=
1
N
∑
k = 1
N
r
k
t
−
r
k
init
2

N is the number of atoms, r
k
(t) are the instantaneous coordinates of atom k and r
k
init are the initial coordinates of atom k.

The structural evolution of the systems was analyzed by means of the radius of gyration Rg defined as the RMSD of the atoms in a molecule from their common center of mass:
R
g
=
∑
k = 1
N
m
k
d
k
2
∑
k = 1
N
m
k

N is the total number of atoms, d
k is the distance of atom k from the center of mass.

The kinetics of H-bond breaking and formation between PNIPAM and water molecules, were investigated by using the Luzar and Chandler [40] model, which expresses the H-bond kinetics as the intermittent hydrogen bond time correlation function, c(t), which is defined as:
c
t
=
h
0
h
t
h
c(t) measures the probability that a hydrogen bond occurring at time t = 0 is still H-bonded at the generic time t; <h> represents the time average of h; h(t) is a hydrogen-bond population operator which is equal to 1 if hydrogen bond is present at time t, otherwise is equal to 0.
Finally, we have used the following geometric criteria for the definition of a hydrogen bond: a) the hydrogen-acceptor distance < 2.5 Å, b) the donor-hydrogen-acceptor angle > 100°.

The CPDs-PNIPAM was prepared following the procedures reported in materials and method sections. The product was purified by dialysis and fully characterized. The morphologic investigation of CPDs core by AFM imaging clearly indicates the presence of spherical shaped particles ( Fig. 1-A). Interestingly, the section analysis evidences a quite homogeneous size distribution of nanoparticles with average size of 23 ± 2 nm (Fig. 1-B, grey arrows) surrounded by a tiny corona shell, about 5 nm in height (Fig. 1-B, yellow arrows) to indicate the presence of a core-shell system. An amount of 5 micrographs were taken for each sample on randomly chosen fields, and the statistical analysis was carried out on more than 75 particles.

The effectiveness core-shell formation through the surface functionalization by PNIPAM units was demonstrated by 1H NMR experiments performed in water solution containing 50% v/v of D2O, at different pH and temperature values (SI). As expected, 1H NMR spectrum of functionalized CPDs-PNIPAM, recorded at 27 °C and neutral pH value, shows the signals relative to the resonance of free PNIPAM. All resonance signals of the core-shell system are compatible with those reported in the literature for the free PNIPAM [23]. Noteworthy, the signals relative to the exchangeable amide protons N-H are observed in the range of 7.25–8.25 ppm with a decrease of intensity (about 50%) id compared to the other signals. Additionally, a downfield shift for all PNIPAM-shell signals was revealed by the increasing of the temperature from 27 °C to 40 °C, according to the coil-to-globule c transition of PNIPAM. For comparison, 1H NMR spectra of PNIPAM (2 mg/mL) in water at temperature below (27 °C) and above (40 °C) the LCST value and at neutral and acid condition (pH v5.0) were also recorded. As expected, the data at neutral pH show the decreasing of N-H signal due to the fast proton exchanges for both temperatures investigated (SI).

The UV-Vis optical absorption spectra of CPDs-PNIPAM exhibited the typically absorption bands for aromatic nitrogen doped CPDs. The first centered at 275 nm which referred to ππ* transition originated from sp2 carbon and the second well-defined band centered at 360 nm, related to the nπ* transition generated from CC and CN bonds [41–43] (Fig. 1C). Contrary, the optical absorption spectra for undoped-CDs appears very unstructured without well-defined peaks [44], as also confirmed by simulated absorption bands (SI). Our simulation data demonstrate the contribute of N-doping on optical absorption spectra. Indeed, as expected for N-doped CPDs the nπ* band is red-shifted of about 30–40 nm respect the undoped CDs (SI). To corroborate our finding, the excitation spectra for CPDs-PNIPAM reports the diagnostic bands at 360 nm and 270 nm (SI). For comparison, optical absorption spectra for CPDs and PNIPAM were measured in water and the data shows a strong absorption at wavelength below 250 nm, which is typical for the PNIPAM chains (SI).

The Fig. 1D depicted the optical emission spectrum for CPDs-PNIPAM at various excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 420 nm. The CPDs-PNIPAM emits strong blue luminescence upon the excitation within both high energy UV absorption band (300 nm; π→π * ) and low energy UV absorption band (350–450 nm; n→π * ). With increasing excitation wavelength, CPDs-PNIPAM shows an excitation dependent emission. The emission peak redshifts and its emission intensity drop in accord with the decreasing absorption. Moreover, we found that for the core-shell nanosystem the fluorescence emission of CPDs core (associated to surface or defect states of CPDs [45–49]) is partially quenched by the PNIPAM shell (SI). This is a clearly indication of the successful functionalization of CPDs surface by PNIPAM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic light scattering and Z-potential characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The DLS measurements at 25 °C show for the CPDs-PNIPAM nano-system a hydrodynamic size value of 600 ± 20 nm at neutral pH and hydrodynamic size value of 640 ± 19 nm at acid pH (pH value5.0). These values, higher than the core-shell size measured with AFM technique, indicate the formation of CPDs-PNIPAM core-shell aggregates.

Noteworthy, at acid pH the hydrodynamic size for CPDs-PNIPAM aggregates drops down with the increasing of the temperature, according to coil-to-globule transition of PNIPAM shell as indicated by our MD simulations and as reported in the literature for similar core-shell nano-system [50,51]. Generally, the acid pH provokes a higher stabilization of globule-conformation than the coil-conformation, favoring the coil-to-globule transition. These data also indicate the absence of inter-cross nanoparticles coagulation [23]. In details, at pH = 5.0 CPDs-PNIPAM retains its hydrodynamic size value (640 ± 19 nm) until the temperature remains below the LCST (< 33 °C). In this condition the PNIPAM shell adopts a well-hydrated “extended-coil conformation” [50] while at temperature above LCST (> 33 °C) the PNIPAM adopt a “collapsed-globule conformation” [50] with a net decreases of hydrodynamic size values of about 480 nm at 35 °C and about 310 nm at 40 °C ( Fig. 2A), as confirmed by the 1H NMR downshifted signals observed from 27 °C to 40 °C (SI). As to the core-shell system this behavior, is less evident at neutral pH, where the hydrodynamic size values are quite constant in the range of between 580 and 700 nm. By increasing the amount of PNIPAM-shell (CPDs: PNIPAM, ratio 1:10) we have observed an evident decreasing of hydrodynamic size of aggregates at acid pH and high temperature (Fig. 2B). While at neutral pH, again no drastically changes were observed. Noteworthy, for CPDs-PNIPAM (ratio 1:10) a shift of the LCST value above 37 °C was observed to confirm the influence of the PNIPAM amount on LCST behavior, as also shown by MD simulations (see Section 3.6). The Fig. 2B reports a scheme of the coil-to-globule transition for the core-shell system from 24 °C to 40 °C.

The Z-potential measurements, give strong evidence of the successful PNIPAM shell formation. Indeed, the Z-potential values for the naked CPDs core does not drastically change from neutral pH (−20.5 ± 3.2 mV) to acid pH (−21.9 ± 3.5 mV), whereas, according to the presence of PNIPAM residues on CPDs surface, it decreases to − 13.5 ± 1.0 mV at neutral pH and to − 0.20 ± 0.05 mV at acid condition, depending on the carbonyl group protonation of PNIPAM (CPDs-PNIPAM ratio 1:3) [52] ( Fig. 3). These data were corroborated by experiments conducted for CPDs-PNIPAM with core-shell weight ratio 1:10. According with the increasing of the PNIPAM amount at CPDs surface, in acid condition the Z-potential reaches the value + 1.03 ± 0.2 mV, to indicates the increase of protonable carbonyl groups into the shell.

We further used the hemolysis assay to evaluate the effect of the CPDs-PNIPAM on the RBCs. No significant hemolytic behavior of the sample can be detected, the percent hemolysis was below 15% for all the concentrations investigated (0.1, 0.6 and 1.1 mg mL−1), confirming the good blood compatibility of CPD-PNIPAM (SI).

Photothermal materials with intense light absorption are necessary to realize photothermal therapy. As shown in Fig. 1a, the as-prepared CPD-PNIPAM show two main optical absorption bands centered at 273 nm (ππ*band) and 360 nm (nπ* band).

The absorption intensity at 404 nm of the as-prepared CPD-PNIPAM is enough high to leads to photothermal conversion efficiency. The light-driven photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of CPD-PNIPAM was measured with the procedure reported in materials and method section. Various concentrations of CPD-PNIPAM were exposed to a 404 nm laser (0.187 W cm−2) to evaluate their photothermal effect.

In Fig. 4A is reported the temperature change, monitored by thermo-camera, for an aqueous dispersion of CPD-PNIPAM (2.5 mg mL−1) continuously exposed to a laser source (405 nm, power of 0.185 W). When the temperature of the system reached a steady state (Tmax 42 °C) the laser was shut off. The temperature change during cooling was monitored to confirm the rate of heat transfer of the system (Fig. 4B). The time constant τs was calculated from graph reported in Fig. 4C to be 189.3 ± 2.9 s

Based on the data recorded, the light-driven photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of the CPDs-PNIPAM was calculated to be 67.9 %, which is high enough for an efficiently photothermal triggered drug release. Many photothermal carbon-based nanomaterials are reported in literature, included a recently carbon polymerized dots system developed in our laboratory which shows a similar photothermal conversion efficiency value of 66.1 % [33]. An interesting review recently published by Karina et al. reports many examples of interesting photothermal carbon-based nanomaterials [53].

The CPDs-PNIPAM/doxo adduct formation was demonstrated by optical absorption investigations. The optical absorption spectrum ( Fig. 5A, line 1) compared to the one of empty CPDs-PNIPAM (Fig. 1C) showed both the doxorubicin broad-band at about 480 nm and the typical nπ* band of carbon core dots at 360 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra recorded at excitation wavelengths of 360 nm and 510 nm clearly show the photoluminescence of the CPDs core centered at 420 nm (Fig. 5A line 2) and the fluorescence emission of doxorubicin centered at 575 nm (Fig. 5A line 3).

To assess the photothermal release of doxorubicin from CPDs-PNIPAM/doxo (at pH 5.0) initially, the dispersion was incubated at room temperature and darks condition and the fluorescence signal measurements at various incubation time were recorded. Then the temperature was regulated to 30 °C and doxorubicin release in dark conditions was investigated.

The finding indicates a slight release of doxorubicin at room temperature, while after 15 min at temperature of 30 °C a release rate of about 0.13% min−1 was estimated. After that the CPDs-PNIPAM/doxo dispersion was irradiated with a cw laser (405 nm – 200 mW). Under light stimulation, the temperature of CPDs-PNIPAM/doxo dispersion reaches a maximum value of ca. 41 ± 1 °C in few minutes. Fluorescence measurements performed after various laser exposition time indicate an effectiveness photothermal release of doxorubicin with a release rate of about 1.0% min−1. The laser source was then switched-off, and the temperature of dispersion was stabilized to 30 °C for 25 min, a release rate of about 0.2% min−1 was then obtained. A further laser exposition (405 nm – 200 mW) for 25 min gives a temperature of 43.5 °C and a release rate of about 0.62% min−1 (Fig. 5B).

Our study reports undoubted photothermal-dependent release of doxorubicin from the CPDs-PNIPAM/doxo system, due to the LCST behaviour of the polymer-shell and due to the electrostatic binding of the drug to the polymer chains. Major doxorubicin release was observed upon laser photothermal excitations, about 15% of doxorubicin are released in 15 min of laser irradiation, while just the 3% of doxorubicin release was observed at 30 °C and very slight drug release was observed at room temperature. Compared with similar system, where a drug release of about 1% in 20 min at 43 °C was observed, [51] our system shows highest performances.

The higher acidity of the cancer microenvironment would be beneficial for this application. Photothermal effect combined with high drug release rate (about 15% in 20 min) and the good stability under physiological condition make the CPDs-PNIPAM core-shell system very promising particles for medical applications.

In Fig. 6 are depicted the R g for PNIPAM on core-shell under different conditions of temperature and pH. The small fluctuations observed below LCST (298 K) for all the studied models confirm the stability of PNIPAM, moreover the coil-to-globule transition does not even occur as the pH changes. A stable R g value of about 6.5 nm was calculated for the investigated systems at both neutral and acids pH (Fig. 6a).

A remarkably different trend is found at temperature above LCST ( Fig. 7b) since during the simulation R g decreases, from the initial value of 7.1 nm to a final value of about 5.5 nm. In all cases PNIPAM undergoes a coil-to-globule transition but we observe that protonation facilitates the transition, in fact the R g of protonated CDP6 starts the transition after about 75 ns followed by the neutral models which start the transition later, after about 115 ns. Such results are according to our experimental findings for hydrodynamic size investigation reported in Fig. 2, and more in general the effect due to the acid pH on the PNIPAM transition is well known [23,54]. On the other hand, the coil-to-globule transition observed for CDP6 could be also facilitate by an intense inter-chain attractions which increase as the number of chains increase. Such interactions destabilize the highly cooperative water cluster that, in turns, stabilize the coil form by means of H bonds with the PNIPAM amide groups [50,55]. These data is in good agreement with DLS and Z-potential investigations performed for core-shell system (molar ratio 1:10), the results clearly indicated an increases of LCST with the increasing of PNIPAM-shell units.

We have also examined the overall stability of the studied models by using the RMSD of the trajectories compared to the initial optimized geometries. Fig. 6c-d illustrate the RMSDs for all atoms calculated under different conditions. In the trajectory run at room temperature the values fluctuate between 1.9 and 2.5 Angstrom confirming a close similarity compared to the starting geometry, hence, according to both R g and hydrodynamic radius, no transition takes place. Differently, above LCST the RMSDs increases of about 2.5 Angstrom during the simulation, reaching a maximum value of about 4.3 Angstrom. Consistently with the results found for R g a significant structural change occurs after about 75 ns under acid conditions whereas in neutral condition this modification occurs over a longer simulation time (about 110 ns).

In Fig. 6e-f are displayed the distance fluctuations between doxorubicin and PNIPAM at different conditions of temperature and pH values. A desorption of doxorubicin occurs only above the LCST indicating a weakening of the intermolecular forces after the transition to globule form; this structural change is in fact decisive for the release of the doxorubicin, according to the photothermal release experiments illustrated in Fig. 5B. Also, in this case the role of pH is relevant favoring the doxorubicin desorption in an acid environment with respect the neutral condition. In Fig. 7A are reported the geometries of the CPD6 system under pH condition and 310 K at different time; after 50 ns the linear structure of the chains is maintained and doxorubicin molecules remain adsorbed to the polymer, after 150 ns the folding to a collapsed globular structure promotes the desorption of the drug. In particular electrostatic repulsions between protonated nitrogen atom of doxorubicin and protonated oxygen atoms along the polymer are at the base of the separation. It is interesting to observe the interactions between the individual polymer chains which, as discussed above, facilitate the coil-to-globule transition.

The MD simulations investigations indicate a release of doxorubicin also at neutral condition, although less marked than the acid condition, whereas experimental result clearly show that the doxorubicin release at neutral condition is quite negligible. Finally, we have investigated the kinetic of hydrogen-bond formation between acceptor groups of PNIPAM (OC<) and hydrogen atom donors of solvent molecules. The autocorrelation functions for the H-bond mentioned above are illustrated in Fig. 7B.

It is evident that above the LCST the scale of H-bond persistence is in agreement with the radius of gyration and RMSD results, the lowest effectiveness of the interactions is observed for CPD6 in acid environment which undergoes the transition first, compared to neutral condition, confirming the mainly role of protonation (Figure 8B line green and blue). Below LCST the c(t) functions show a slower decay indicating that the polymer chains form effective hydrogen bond with solvent molecules stabilizing the coil form at 298 K (figure 8B line red and black).

In summary, we have developed a novel luminescent and photothermal-responsive core-shell nanosystem based on carbonized polymer dots CPDs (core) and PNIPAM (shell). The photothermal triggered release of doxorubicin was demonstrated, reaching a release rate of about 1.0% min−1. We found an excellent photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of 67.9%, which are enough for drug release. The molecular dynamic data showed that at temperature of 310 K the coil-to-globule transition occurs promoting the desorption of doxorubicin from the polymer, as confirmed by experimental results. Gyration radius, RMSD and fluctuation of the distance doxorubicin-PNIPAM termination confirm that temperature plays the main role in the folding process whereas the acid pH facilitated the transition. The interactions between the polymer chains on shell, observed during the simulations, suggest that the number of PNIPAM chain covalently linked to the CPDs core affects the LCST behavior. This data was confirmed by DLS investigations, which report an increasing of about 4 units of LCST value for the core-shell with high molar ratio 1:10.

G. Forte: Conceptualization, Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. G. Consiglio: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. L. Maugeri: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. C. Satriano: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. S. Petralia: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research activity has been developed under the INCREASE project, funded under Action Linea Piaceri- STARTING GRANT 2020, University of Catania, Italy. Special thanks to Prof. M. Teresa Musumeci for the use of DLS, Z-potential and spectrofluorometer instruments and to provide the doxorubicin for the photothermal-triggered release experiments.
